headline: Carr's precision offense overwhelms UCLA in Notre Dame's dominant Week 1 statement
body: CJ Carr carved up the UCLA secondary with surgical efficiency, completing 24 of 36 passes for 346 yards and four touchdowns to stake Notre Dame to an early command that the Bruins could never seriously challenge. Carr's poise in the pocket—particularly his willingness to let plays develop vertically—created openings for receivers like Mcpherson, who broke free for 95 yards on just three catches, including a touchdown that gave Notre Dame breathing room before halftime.

UCLA's Jaylen Iamaleava showed flashes of competence in his own right, racking up 282 passing yards and three touchdowns, and his dual-threat ability kept the Bruins close enough to prevent a complete rout. Yet the Bruins' run game never materialized. With only 77 rushing yards and Iamaleava himself contributing just 34 on the ground, UCLA found itself constantly playing from behind, forced into a pass-heavy approach that played into Notre Dame's hands. Schwesinger's eight tackles kept UCLA's defense busy, but it was a largely reactive effort.

The clearest dividing line was turnover discipline. Notre Dame protected the football completely while UCLA coughed it up once, a margin that reflected the overall gap in execution. Notre Dame's defense, led by Middleton's five tackles, kept UCLA's offense honest without generating overwhelming pressure, which speaks to the quality of Carr's management. The Irish never needed a sack to slow down the Bruins. They simply executed their gameplan with the kind of precision that suggests this team entered the season further along developmentally than most Week 1 opponents.
turningPoint: Mcpherson's 95-yard reception and touchdown haul established Notre Dame's vertical passing game early, forcing UCLA into one-dimensional play and turning what could have been competitive into a rout by the third quarter.

unsungHero.name: Price
unsungHero.why: His 17 carries for 78 yards and a touchdown kept UCLA's defense honest and prevented the Bruins from stacking the box entirely against Carr, yet gets overshadowed by the quarterback's dominant performance
